modernLegacy.culturalImpact: The Yasakani no Magatama, as one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan, holds profound cultural significance that extends far beyond its historical and mythological origins. Magatama jewels themselves are potent symbols of ancient Japan, evidenced by their ubiquitous presence in archaeological sites throughout the archipelago. Their discovery in burial mounds and other elite contexts underscores their association with power, status, and the afterlife. In modern times, the Yasakani no Magatama retains its status as a national symbol, albeit one shrouded in mystery. Unlike the Yata no Kagami, which is enshrined at Ise Grand Shrine, the Yasakani no Magatama is kept within the Imperial Palace in Tokyo, making it technically more accessible yet simultaneously more elusive. Its concealment from public view has only amplified its mystique, giving rise to numerous theories and speculations regarding its precise appearance, composition, and even its very nature. Some scholars and commentators posit that the 'jewel' is not a single, monolithic object, but rather a string or necklace composed of multiple magatama beads. This interpretation aligns with archaeological findings, where magatama are frequently discovered in clusters or as part of elaborate necklaces. The enduring fascination with the Yasakani no Magatama is reflected in its pervasive presence in contemporary Japanese art, literature, and popular culture. It serves as a recurring motif in anime, manga, video games, and other forms of media, often imbued with symbolic weight and magical properties. The jewel's distinctive comma shape has also inspired modern jewelry designs, serving as a tangible link to Japan's rich cultural heritage. The magatama, therefore, stands as a testament to the enduring power of mythology to shape cultural identity and inspire artistic expression across generations.

worshipPractices: The Yasakani no Magatama is deeply intertwined with the veneration of the Imperial line and the ideals of benevolent rule. As a tangible representation of the Emperor's compassion and concern for his people, it embodies the virtue of *jin* (仁), a core concept in Confucian ethics that emphasizes humaneness, empathy, and altruism. The magatama itself has been regarded as a sacred object in Japan since prehistoric times, predating even the formalization of Shinto as a distinct religious tradition. Various interpretations have been offered regarding the symbolic meaning of the magatama's curved shape. Some scholars suggest that it represents the human soul, reflecting its journey through life and the afterlife. Others propose a lunar symbolism, associating the magatama with the waxing and waning phases of the moon and its connection to fertility and cyclical renewal. Another common interpretation links the magatama to the form of a fetus, symbolizing life, birth, and the continuity of generations. The comma shape can also be seen as representing the principle of yin and yang, the complementary forces that govern the universe and maintain cosmic balance. In the context of the Three Imperial Regalia, the Yasakani no Magatama complements the Yata no Kagami (mirror) and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (sword), forming a trinity of virtues essential for just and effective governance: wisdom, valor, and benevolence. The jewel, therefore, serves as a constant reminder of the Emperor's duty to rule with compassion and to prioritize the well-being of his subjects.

festivals.description: The jewel was hung on the sakaki tree, alongside the mirror, to entice Amaterasu from her self-imposed exile within the Ama-no-Iwato cave, restoring light and order to the world.
festivals.name: Cave of Amaterasu (Ama-no-Iwato)

festivals.description: The Yasakani no Magatama is a central element in the Imperial Succession ceremonies, formally presented to the new Emperor as a symbol of his legitimacy and his inheritance of the Imperial virtues.
festivals.name: Imperial Succession (Sokui no Rei)

festivals.description: The Yasakani no Magatama is carefully guarded within the Imperial Palace, where daily rites and ceremonies are performed to honor the Imperial ancestors and ensure the well-being of the nation. The specific details of these rites are closely guarded secrets.
festivals.name: Daily Imperial Rites (Kashikodokoro)

cognates.notes: Possible Chinese borrowing or cognate, though specific etymological links require further investigation.

materials: Possibly jade (nephrite or jadeite), agate, quartz, or other sacred stone. Jade is the most commonly cited material.
name: Yasakani no Magatama

extendedContent.title: Description and Nature
extendedContent.content:
The Yasakani no Magatama (八尺瓊勾玉), often translated as 'Eight-Shaku Curved Jewel,' is one of the Sanshu no Jingi (三種の神器), the Three Sacred Treasures or Imperial Regalia of Japan. These three objects – the Yasakani no Magatama, the Yata no Kagami (八咫鏡, 'Eight-Hand Mirror'), and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙剣, 'Grass-Cutting Sword') – are deeply symbolic and represent the legitimacy and authority of the Japanese Imperial line. According to Shinto mythology, the Imperial family is directly descended from Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess, and the regalia serve as tangible proof of this divine lineage.
                
                
                    The term *magatama* refers to a distinctive type of curved, comma-shaped bead or jewel with a hole drilled through one end. These objects have been discovered in archaeological sites throughout Japan, dating back to the Jōmon period (c. 14,000 – 300 BCE), indicating their antiquity and cultural significance. The Yasakani no Magatama is believed to be crafted from jade or another precious stone, though its exact composition remains a matter of speculation due to its secluded location within the Imperial Palace and its lack of public display. Some scholars suggest that the 'jewel' may not be a single bead but rather a necklace composed of multiple magatama strung together. The term 'eight-shaku' (*yasakani*) could refer to the length of such a necklace, although this interpretation remains contested.

                    The material of the magatama is also significant. Jade, in particular, is often associated with longevity, immortality, and spiritual power in East Asian cultures. Its green color symbolizes life, growth, and harmony with nature, further reinforcing the Yasakani no Magatama's connection to the vitality and prosperity of the Imperial line and the nation as a whole.

The myth of the Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, 'Heavenly Rock Cave') is central to understanding the origin and significance of the Yasakani no Magatama. According to the *Kojiki* (古事記, 'Record of Ancient Matters') and the *Nihon Shoki* (日本書紀, 'Chronicles of Japan'), Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess, retreated into the Ama-no-Iwato cave after being deeply offended by the disruptive and violent behavior of her brother, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, the storm god.
                
                
                    With Amaterasu concealed within the cave, the world was plunged into darkness and chaos. The other deities, known as the *kami*, gathered to devise a plan to entice Amaterasu to emerge and restore light to the world. The deity Tamanooya-no-Mikoto (玉祖命), traditionally associated with jewel-making, is credited with crafting the Yasakani no Magatama for this purpose. The deities hung the newly created Yasakani no Magatama, along with the Yata no Kagami, on a sacred *sakaki* tree (榊) outside the cave entrance. Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto (天宇受賣命), the goddess of dance and merriment, performed a provocative and energetic dance that caused the assembled *kami* to erupt in boisterous laughter.
                
                
                    Intrigued by the commotion, Amaterasu cautiously peered out of the cave entrance. She saw her own radiant reflection in the Yata no Kagami, and the combined allure of the mirror and the beautiful Yasakani no Magatama drew her forth from the cave. The *kami* quickly sealed the entrance, preventing her from retreating again. Light returned to the world, and order was restored. The Yasakani no Magatama and the Yata no Kagami, having played crucial roles in this pivotal event, became sacred treasures, forever associated with Amaterasu and the restoration of cosmic harmony.

                Descent to Earth (Tenson Kōrin)
                
                    Later, when Amaterasu sent her grandson, Ninigi-no-Mikoto (瓊瓊杵尊), to descend from Takamagahara (高天原, 'High Plain of Heaven') and rule the terrestrial realm, she bestowed upon him the Three Sacred Treasures as symbols of his divine authority and mandate. These regalia served as proof of his lineage and legitimacy, establishing the foundation for the Imperial line that continues to the present day. The Yasakani no Magatama, therefore, represents not only benevolence and compassion but also the divine right to rule, passed down through generations of Emperors.

extendedContent.title: Historical Significance
extendedContent.content:
The Yasakani no Magatama occupies a unique position among the Three Imperial Regalia. While the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is traditionally housed at Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, and the Yata no Kagami is enshrined at Ise Grand Shrine, the Yasakani no Magatama is kept within the Kashikodokoro (賢所), the central sanctuary within the Imperial Palace in Tokyo. This location underscores its close association with the Emperor and the Imperial family. The regalia are carefully guarded and are not typically displayed to the public.
                
                
                    The Yasakani no Magatama plays a vital role in the Sokui no Rei (即位の礼), the enthronement ceremony of the Japanese Emperor. During this ceremony, the regalia, including the Yasakani no Magatama, are formally presented to the new Emperor, signifying the transfer of Imperial authority and the continuity of the Imperial line. The presence of the regalia serves as a powerful symbol of the Emperor's legitimacy and his connection to the divine origins of Japan.
                
                
                    Unlike the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, which, according to legend, was lost at sea during the Genpei War in 1185 (and may have been replaced with a replica), and the Yata no Kagami, which is kept at Ise Grand Shrine and is therefore inaccessible, the Yasakani no Magatama is believed by some to be the original artifact crafted during the age of the gods. Its continued presence within the Imperial Palace represents an unbroken link to Japan's ancient past and the enduring power of its mythology. The mystery surrounding its appearance and composition only adds to its allure and cultural significance.

firstAttestation.description: Estimated based on the literary tradition of the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, which were compiled during the Asuka and Nara periods.

createdBy: Tamanooya-no-Mikoto, deity of jewel-making, traditionally attributed as the creator of the Yasakani no Magatama.

rituals.description: The jewel was hung on the sakaki tree to entice Amaterasu from hiding, restoring light and order to the world. This event is commemorated in various Shinto rituals and festivals.
rituals.name: Cave of Amaterasu (Ama-no-Iwato)

rituals.description: The jewel is presented to the new Emperor during succession ceremonies, symbolizing the transfer of Imperial authority and the continuity of the Imperial line.
rituals.name: Imperial Succession (Sokui no Rei)

rituals.description: The jewel is kept in the Imperial Palace where daily rites are performed, honoring the Imperial ancestors and ensuring the well-being of the nation. The specifics of these rites are not publicly known.
rituals.name: Daily Imperial Rites (Kashikodokoro)

textReferences.context: The *Kojiki* recounts the creation of the jewel by Tamanooya-no-Mikoto and its role in enticing Amaterasu from the cave.
The *Nihon Shoki* provides a similar account of the jewel's crafting and its significance in the Ama-no-Iwato myth.
The *Kojiki* describes how the jewel, along with the other regalia, was given to Ninigi-no-Mikoto as part of his mandate to rule the terrestrial realm.

mythologyContexts.symbolism: The Yasakani no Magatama is imbued with rich symbolic meaning. It primarily represents *jin* (仁), benevolence and compassion, essential virtues for a righteous and effective ruler. This symbolism underscores the Emperor's duty to care for his people and govern with empathy and understanding. Beyond this primary meaning, the curved shape of the magatama itself has been interpreted in various ways. Some scholars suggest it represents the journey of the human soul, reflecting its cyclical path through life and death. Others propose a connection to the principle of yin and yang, symbolizing the unity of opposing forces and the balance of the universe. Still others see the comma shape as representing a fetus, symbolizing life, birth, and the continuity of generations. The material of the magatama, often believed to be jade, further reinforces its symbolic weight. Jade is associated with longevity, immortality, and spiritual power, reflecting the enduring nature of the Imperial line and its connection to the divine. The color green, traditionally associated with jade, symbolizes life, growth, and harmony with nature. If the Yasakani no Magatama is indeed a necklace composed of multiple beads, as some sources suggest, this could symbolize the unity of the people under a single, benevolent ruler.
mythologyContexts.culturalSignificance: The cultural significance of the Yasakani no Magatama is deeply rooted in its role as one of the Three Imperial Regalia. These objects are not merely historical artifacts; they are potent symbols of Japanese national identity, representing the legitimacy and continuity of the Imperial line. The magatama, in particular, serves as a tangible link to Japan's ancient past, connecting the modern nation to its mythological origins. The discovery of magatama in archaeological sites throughout Japan, dating back thousands of years, underscores their enduring importance as symbols of power, status, and spiritual significance. The Yasakani no Magatama's presence within the Imperial Palace, while shrouded in secrecy, further amplifies its mystique and cultural weight. Its concealment from public view has only fueled speculation and fascination, contributing to its enduring status as a national treasure. The jewel's image and symbolism are frequently invoked in Japanese art, literature, and popular culture, serving as a reminder of the nation's rich mythological heritage. The magatama has become a recognizable emblem of Japanese culture, representing its history, traditions, and spiritual values.
mythologyContexts.usage: The Yasakani no Magatama represents the virtue of benevolence and the Emperor's compassion for the people. Magatama jewels have been sacred in Japan since prehistoric times, possibly representing the human soul, the moon, or a fetus. The comma shape may symbolize the cycle of life and death, yin and yang, or the unity of heaven and earth. As one of the Three Sacred Treasures, it completes the trinity of Imperial virtues: wisdom (mirror), valor (sword), and benevolence (jewel). It is used in the Imperial Succession ceremonies, signifying the transfer of power and legitimacy.

longDescription: Yasakani no Magatama (八尺瓊勾玉, 'Eight-Shaku Curved Jewel') is the sacred jewel and one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan (Sanshu no Jingi). Magatama are comma-shaped or curved beads that have been found in Japan dating back to prehistoric times, often made of jade, agate, or quartz. The Yasakani no Magatama was created by the deity Tamanooya-no-Mikoto to help lure Amaterasu from the Heavenly Rock Cave (Ama-no-Iwato). Along with the mirror (Yata no Kagami), it was hung on the sacred sakaki tree outside the cave. The jewel represents benevolence and compassion, the third Imperial virtue alongside wisdom (mirror) and valor (sword). It was given to Ninigi-no-Mikoto when he descended from Takamagahara and has been passed down through the Imperial line. The jewel is currently kept in the Imperial Palace in Tokyo, in the Sword and Jewel Sanctuaries (Kashikodokoro). Like the other regalia, it has never been publicly displayed, and its exact appearance, size, and material composition remain uncertain. The term 'eight-shaku' (yasakani) is an ancient Japanese measurement, with one shaku being approximately one foot. However, it is debated whether this refers to the length of the jewel itself or the length of a string of magatama beads. Some scholars believe the Yasakani no Magatama is a single, large jade bead, while others suggest it is a necklace composed of multiple smaller magatama. The absence of photographic or detailed descriptions makes it impossible to definitively resolve this question. The Yasakani no Magatama embodies the Confucian virtue of *jin*, representing the Emperor's commitment to the well-being of his people and his responsibility to rule with compassion and empathy. As a symbol of benevolence, it serves as a constant reminder of the moral obligations associated with Imperial power.
symbolism: The Yasakani no Magatama is the only one of the three regalia kept in the Imperial Palace rather than a Shinto shrine. It is traditionally used during the enthronement ceremony (Sokui no rei) as one of the objects formally transferred to the new emperor. Unlike the sword (which was legendarily lost at sea in 1185 during the Genpei War and may have been replaced) and the mirror (which is kept at Ise), the magatama is believed to be the original artifact from the age of the gods, never lost or destroyed throughout Japan's turbulent history. The material of the magatama, most likely jade, contributes significantly to its symbolism. Jade is highly valued in East Asian cultures for its beauty, durability, and perceived spiritual properties. It is associated with longevity, prosperity, and good fortune, further enhancing the Yasakani no Magatama's status as a sacred and auspicious object. The color green, often associated with jade, symbolizes life, growth, and harmony with nature, connecting the jewel to the vital forces that sustain the world.

images.caption: Artistic depiction of the sacred curved jewel, often imagined as a large, green jade magatama.

description:
The Yasakani no Magatama is a central artifact in Japanese mythology and history, revered as one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan, alongside the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword and the Yata no Kagami mirror. The magatama is traditionally described as a curved jewel, often rendered in jade, although other materials such as agate and jasper have also been used. Its shape, resembling a comma or a stylized animal tooth, is believed to have ancient origins, possibly connected to prehistoric animistic beliefs.

Symbolically, the Yasakani no Magatama represents benevolence, gentleness, and virtue. As part of the Imperial Regalia, it embodies the emperor's responsibility to rule with compassion and justice. The jewel signifies the emperor's role as a benevolent leader, ensuring the well-being and prosperity of the nation. Its presence alongside the sword (representing valor) and the mirror (representing wisdom) highlights the balanced qualities expected of a Japanese monarch.

Historically, the Yasakani no Magatama is associated with the goddess Amaterasu, the sun goddess. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the earliest written records of Japanese history and mythology, Amaterasu hid in a cave, plunging the world into darkness. To lure her out, the other gods crafted the Yasakani no Magatama and the Yata no Kagami. When Amaterasu saw her reflection in the mirror and was intrigued by the jewel, she emerged from the cave, restoring light to the world. This narrative underscores the jewel's significance in restoring order and balance.
powers: Mystical properties, traditionally associated with good fortune, protection, and the continuity of the Imperial line.

Symbol of life and immortality, representing a benevolent force, paralleling the Yasakani no Magatama's association with prosperity and well-being.
A circular jade artifact representing heaven and virtue, akin to the Yasakani no Magatama's association with benevolence and cosmic order.
